coredata.dc:title: Novel Hg (II) selective fluorescent green sensor based on carbon dots synthesized from starch and functionalized with methimazole
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2021 The Authors. Published by Elsevier Inc.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Authors. Published by Elsevier Inc.
coredata.dc:creator: Vieira, Maria Carolina Robaina
Carvalho, Felipe Andrade
Franco, Mathews Krambeck de Oliveira
Suarez, Willian Toito
Vilanculo, Castelo Bandane
dos Santos, Marcelo Henrique
Gambarra-Neto, Francisco Fernandes
coredata.dc:description: We describe a green new method for the synthesis of water-soluble photoluminescent carbon dots (CDs) that were functionalized with methimazole (MTZ) and applied to determine Hg2+ based on the fluorescence extinction. Starch obtained from rice was used as a natural source for the production of CDs by hydrothermal treatment. Also, it was proposed a factorial design to optimize the parameters for CD synthesis and the results showed that the luminescence intensity is a function of temperature and not of the heating time in the hydrothermal process. The synthesized CDs were characterized using fluorescence techniques, Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR), and UV–Vis spectroscopy. Through transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and dynamic light scattering (DLS), it was found the formation of CDs on a nanometer scale with an average size of 11 nm. The functionalization with MTZ, eliminated all interferences from other metals, indicating a selective response to Hg2+ ions. The method was applied to Hg2+ determination in waters. Under optimal conditions, was obtained a limit of detection of 1.8 × 10−7 mol L−1 with a linear range from 3.3 × 10−7 to 50.0 × 10−6 mol L−1. Therefore, the proposed method can be considered a simple, selective, and precise alternative that minimizes the number of reagents used for Hg2+ determination in natural waters, and can be applied on a large scale in environmental analyzes.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Mercury(II)
Methimazole
Starch
Water

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Fluorescence detection of Hg2+ employing CDs/MTZ
Analysis of experimental planning for CDs synthesis
FTIR CDs, FTIR MTZ and FTIR CDs/MTZ
Application of CDs/MTZ in Hg2+ determination
Comparison with other methods to determine Hg2+

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Effect of the interaction between temperature and time on the luminescence intensity of the CDs, as a 3D response surface.
FTIR spectra for MTZ (A), CDs/MTZ (B) and CDs (C).
Difference between the relative luminescence intensities of the (A) blank and CDs (B) blank and CDs/MTZ with different metal ions in the concentration of 5.0 × 10−3 mol L−1 in pH 4.0.
Schematic illustration of the probable functionalization of the surface of the CDs and possible interaction of CDs/MTZ with Hg2+.

Fluorescence spectra of CDs/MTZ in aqueous solution after adding different Hg2+ concentrations (0.33–50.0 µmol L−1). Insertion: linear relation between Hg2+ concentrations from 0.33 µ to 50.0 µmol L−1 and changes in fluorescence intensity. ⅄ex = 360 nm.
Determination of Hg2+ by the suggested fluorescence sensor in the different samples.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: Note: The results are expressed: mean ± standard deviation.

head.ce:title: Novel Hg (II) selective fluorescent green sensor based on carbon dots synthesized from starch and functionalized with methimazole

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Federal University of Viçosa, Viçosa, MG 36570-900, Brazil

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Chemistry, Federal University of Viçosa

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, Federal University of Viçosa, Viçosa - MG 36570-900, Brazil

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Brazilian Enterprise for Agricultural Research, Campina Grande, PB 58428-095, Brazil
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Brazilian Enterprise for Agricultural Research, Campina Grande 58428-095, PB, Brazil

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Correspondence to: Centro de Ciências Exatas e Tecnológicas, Universidade Federal de Viçosa, Avenida Peter Henry Rolphs, s/n, Viçosa, MG 36570-900, Brazil.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Centro de Ciências Exatas e Tecnológicas, Universidade Federal de Viçosa

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: We describe a green new method for the synthesis of water-soluble photoluminescent carbon dots (CDs) that were functionalized with methimazole (MTZ) and applied to determine Hg2+ based on the fluorescence extinction. Starch obtained from rice was used as a natural source for the production of CDs by hydrothermal treatment. Also, it was proposed a factorial design to optimize the parameters for CD synthesis and the results showed that the luminescence intensity is a function of temperature and not of the heating time in the hydrothermal process. The synthesized CDs were characterized using fluorescence techniques, Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR), and UV–Vis spectroscopy. Through transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and dynamic light scattering (DLS), it was found the formation of CDs on a nanometer scale with an average size of 11 nm. The functionalization with MTZ, eliminated all interferences from other metals, indicating a selective response to Hg2+ ions. The method was applied to Hg2+ determination in waters. Under optimal conditions, was obtained a limit of detection of 1.8 × 10−7 mol L−1 with a linear range from 3.3 × 10−7 to 50.0 × 10−6 mol L−1. Therefore, the proposed method can be considered a simple, selective, and precise alternative that minimizes the number of reagents used for Hg2+ determination in natural waters, and can be applied on a large scale in environmental analyzes.

ce:section.ce:para: In recent years, an important task in environmental analysis is the determination of heavy metal ions in water samples for human consumption. Heavy metal ions are environmental pollutants and harmful to human health, especially mercury(II) ions (Li et al., 2017; Yu et al., 2018). It is one of the most dangerous pollutants, with strong toxicity and bioaccumulation (Nolan and Lippard, 2008; Wang et al., 2013; Zhang and Chen, 2013), which can lead to irreversible damage to human health due to its adverse effects on vital organs and tissues, even at low concentrations (Nolan and Lippard, 2008; Saleem et al., 2017; Zhang et al., 2017).

Several methods have been developed for the determination of trace mercury, such as cold vapor atomic absorption spectroscopy (CV-AAS), inductively coupled plasma optical emission spectroscopy (ICP-OES) (Hsu et al., 2013; Jarzyńska and Falandysz, 2011), mass spectrometry (MS) (Leermakers et al., 2005), colorimetric detection (Wang et al., 2020c), gas chromatography (GC) (Arancibia et al., 2003), high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) (Cheng et al., 2015), a boric acid-Functionalized lanthanide metal-organic framework as a fluorescence “turn-on” (Wang et al., 2020a), among others. Although these approaches offer good performance for Hg2+ detection, in most cases, these methods require expensive and sophisticated instrumentation, complex sample processing, besides a high-cost analysis limits their routine application (Li et al., 2011; Nolan and Lippard, 2008; Wang et al., 2013; Zhang and Chen, 2013).

Methods such as ICP-MS have sensitivity in the order of ng L−1 due to the presence of sophisticated components, entailing a high cost per analysis and maintenance, making it unfeasible in many cases. Therefore, there is a need to obtain low-cost, fast (Nolan and Lippard, 2008) and highly sensitive methods for Hg2+ detection in aqueous solution, which facilitates environmental monitoring and clinical toxicology (Liang et al., 2015; Rooney, 2007). This demand can be answered by ion detection by fluorescent sensors, which have a fast response, simple handling, low cost, high sensitivity, and simplicity (Wang et al., 2020b).

Carbon dots (CDs) are important nanoparticles in the fluorescent sensor class, which have attracted considerable attention since their discovery in 2004 (Xu et al., 2004). There is an increasing interest in this carbon material, due to its promising properties, such as high chemical stability, good photostability, low toxicity, simple synthesis, and high solubility in water (Choi et al., 2018; Sachdev and Gopinath, 2015). Consequently, CDs have found many applications in the research field, including bioimaging, electrocatalysis (Weng et al., 2018), photocatalysis (Liu and Chen, 2013) and sensors for metals (Ke et al., 2017) and organic substances (Liao et al., 2019).

CDs synthesis from natural biomass has been growing in the field of nanotechnology, with reports of non-toxic, inexhaustible, and inexpensive products (Liu et al., 2017), with applications in various fields, such as energy, analytical detection (Dong et al., 2012) and in functionalized materials (Salas et al., 2010).

Many synthetic methods have already been developed for perfect CD functionalization, with doping and surface functionalization being the two most important to control the chemical, optical and electrical performance of CDs (Wang and Qiu, 2016). CDs surface is easy to handle, due to their composition and the presence of abundant functional groups, which makes them sensitive to the environment pH (Xia et al., 2019), solvent (Liu et al., 2019), ions (Lu et al., 2019), small biological and chemical molecules, for example, enabling the formation of CDs with different properties from the previous ones (Wang and Qiu, 2016).

There is a continuous and lasting interest in research and improvement of methods for determining metals in biological and environmental systems. In particular, some metal ions attract more interest due to their involvement and presence in human life, and their respective impacts, both positive and negative. In addition to being able to be synthesized by various techniques and from different natural and renewable sources, the fact that they are non-toxic also improves the CDs potential without impacting health and the environment.

This work proposes the development of a green sensor synthesized from rice starch, highly luminescent and soluble in water, functionalized with methimazole for selective Hg2+ determination in natural waters.

As far as we know, for the first time, a process for functionalizing CDs using methimazole was described. Another novelty is the synthesis of CDs through starch obtained from rice washing water. Works published so far involving CD synthesis with rice, are generally carried out through the husk of the cereal (Heng et al., 2020; Thongsai et al., 2019; Zainal Abidin et al., 2020). However, the vast majority of commercialized rice has no husk, which restricts the application of rice husk in the synthesis of CDs. Furthermore, the synthesis of carbon dots through rice washing water is simpler and easier to obtain, besides that, this residue would have no other purpose. These are the properties that bring innovations when compared to previously published works in the literature.

The solutions were prepared with ultrapure water (resistivity > 18.0 MΩ cm) obtained from a Millipore Milli-Q system (USA). Methimazole (1-methyl-3H-imidazole-2-thione), Mercury (II) chloride, sodium hydroxide, and hydrochloric acid 37% (w/v) were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich and used without further purification. The stock solution of Hg2+ 5.0 × 10−3 mol L−1 was prepared by dissolving 1.36 g of HgCl2 in 1000 mL of distilled water. The solutions of ions Al3+, Ba2+, Cd2+, Cr3+, Mn2+, Ni2+, Zn2+, Cu2+, Ca2+, Fe2+, Fe3+, Ag+, Cl-, NO3 – and SO4 2– (interferents) were prepared from their nitrate salts or chloride salts of analytical grade. The white rice used in the synthesis of carbon dots was purchased at a supermarket in Viçosa-MG-Brazil. The tap water sample was collected from our research laboratory and the lake, pond, and river waters samples were collected in the city of Viçosa-MG-Brazil. The samples were collected in three different points being subsequently filtered with a low pore diameter filter (0.22 µm) and stored in polyethylene bottles. For the analysis of the samples, the pH was adjusted in 4.0 using a pH meter.

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) image was recorded on an EM 109 transmission electron microscope (Zeiss, Germany). Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) experiments were conducted on a Spectrum 1000 FTIR spectrometer (Perkin-Elmer, USA) for the characterization of the CDs functional groups. The particle size of the synthesized nanoparticles was measured by dynamic light scattering (DLS) employing a Zetasizer Nano S (Malvern, England) in which a 90° dispersion angle was used. The pH was monitored on a pH benchtop meter (Mettler Toledo, EUA). Model LF0910 (JUNG, Brazil) muffle furnace was employed for hydrothermal treatment. UV-Vis absorption spectra were recorded on a Lambda 950 UV–Vis spectrophotometer (Perkin-Elmer, USA) with a 1.0-cm quartz cuvette at room temperature. Fluorescence emission spectra were recorded on an AvaSpec-2048L fluorescence spectrophotometer (Avantes, Netherlands) using a quartz cuvette with four polished sides and 10 mm of path length.

CDs were synthesized from the rice starch via hydrothermal treatment on a muffle furnace. White rice was left to stand for 5 min in water (1:2/rice: water) for carbon dots preparation from a natural source. At the end of that time, whitish water was obtained indicating the presence of starch. Then a stainless-steel autoclave with the inner part composed of a Teflon container with 25 mL capacity was employed. About 20 mL of the starch solution was added to the Teflon container, and then it was inserted into the autoclave, which was subjected to constant heating of 230 °C for 6 h. Later, the autoclave was cooled at room temperature and a uniform brown liquid was obtained, which indicated the formation of water-soluble CDs. The solution was left to stand for 3 h to decant coarse particles and then filtered through a quantitative filter. The aqueous solution was filtered again with a low pore diameter filter (0.22 nm). The executed protocol is shown in Fig. S1.

The quantum fluorescence efficiency of the CDs was calculated by comparing their integrated fluorescence intensities (360 nm) and absorbance values (360 nm) with those of quinine sulfate48, according to the following equation:
(1)
ϕ =
ϕ
R
+
I
I
R
+
A
R
A
+
η
2
η
R
2

where I, A, and ɳ are the measured integrated emission intensity, the optical density, and the refractive index of the solvent, respectively. The subscripts R denote a pattern (quinine sulfate with known quantum fluorescence efficiency of 0.54) and ɸ is the quantum efficiency of fluorescence.

The first stage of optimization is the choice of variables that can significantly influence the analytical response (Cao et al., 2007). Temperature and time are the most influential factors in the CDs synthesis. A set of experiments was carried out to study the effect of each of these factors, based on the luminescence of CDs. A central quadratic composite planning of 2 variables with 11 experiments, 3 central ones, was performed to obtain the Response Surface Methodology (RSM), a statistical method used to assess the relationship between the independent variables (temperature and time) and thus determine the best conditions for the production of highly luminescent CDs. Table S1 shows the factors and their studied levels. All calculations used in the analysis of the experimental design were performed using the software Statistica 8.0.

The functionalization of the CDs obtained from the starch via hydrothermal treatment took place in two stages, in the first stage the activation occurred to make the surface of the nanostructure more reactive to functionalization, so, 10 mL of 0.1 mol L−1 NaOH solution was added to 10 mL of the CD solution and refluxed for 1 h. In the second stage, the addition of functional groups to the CDs surface was carried out using 0.1 g of MTZ that was added to the obtained solution and placed under reflux for 1 h. Finally, the solution was washed with ethyl acetate to remove excess MTZ and then filtered through a pore diameter filter (0.22 µm).

To verify the selectivity of the Hg2+ determination method from CDs functionalized with MTZ, a study was carried out in which several ions under identical conditions were tested in a 1:100 ratio of analyte/interferent and recording the effects on the fluorescence response. The ions evaluated were: Al3+, Ba2+, Cd2+, Cr3+, Mn2+, Cu2+, Ni2+, Zn2+, Ca2+ Fe2+, Fe3+, Ag+, Cl–, NO3 – and SO4 2–.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence detection of Hg2+ employing CDs/MTZ
ce:section.ce:para: The dilution of CDs/MTZ in distilled water was studied in order to increase the intensity of the luminescent signal. According to the results, the best dilution was 150 µL of CDs/MTZ to 2.85 mL of distilled water. For the Hg2+ luminescence determination, 100 µL of the diluted CDs/MTZ solution was transferred to a quartz cuvette, along with the 1900 µL water or sample (both pH adjusted in 4.0) and different concentrations of standard Hg2+ solution, according to the desired concentration values. The waiting time was 5 min for the interaction of the particles and, shortly thereafter, the emission spectrum of each sample was collected at a 360 nm excitation wavelength.

Recovery studies were carried out at three concentration levels of the analytical curve (4, 17, and 30 µmol L−1) to evaluate the accuracy of the proposed method in water samples obtained from the lake, tap, river, and pond. The recovery percentage was calculated according to Eq. (2).
(2)
R ecovery
(
%
)
=
C
exp
C
add
x
100

where C
exp is the average concentration of the analyte determined experimentally in the fortified samples and C
add is the concentration of the analyte added in the non-fortified sample.
The recovery is related to the accuracy of the method, showing the amount of the analyte recovered compared to the actual amount present in the sample (BRITO et al., 2003).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analysis of experimental planning for CDs synthesis
ce:section.ce:para:
The algebraic model for predicting the optimization of CDs is presented in Eq. (3).
(3) Y = 57,334.61 – 499.02 X1 + 86.36 X2 + 1.47 X1 2 + 3.17 X2 2 – 0.77 X1X2
Y being the luminescence intensity, X1, and X2 the temperature and time factors, respectively.

Table S2 presents the results of ANOVA for the synthesis of CDs. The second-order regression model for CDs was found with a significantly higher confidence level (95%). All predicted coefficients were estimated by ANOVA analysis with a highly significant p-value (p < 0,05) for temperature and an R2 = 0.99409, which indicates the highest validity for the predicted luminescence values. A relatively high value of the adjusted R2 coefficients was obtained (adjusted R2 = 0.98819), which indicates that the final prediction is in agreement with the experimental results.

It is noticeable the high significance of the temperature, both for the linear model and the quadratic model with confidence levels above 95%. Time and the relation between temperature and time were not significant for both models, quadratic, and linear in the confidence interval. Thus, a minimum heating time of 6 h was maintained for hydrothermal synthesis.

RSM is commonly applied to study the effect of operational parameters (input variables) that have the most influence on the interest response (output variable) (Behera et al., 2018). By Fig. 1, it is noticeable the increase in the luminescence intensity of the CDs when the temperature is higher and an insignificant increase when the time is increased. It is concluded that the CDs synthesis via hydrothermal treatment at a temperature of 230 °C results in greater luminescence intensity, regardless of time.

Morphology and size of the CDs were evaluated by TEM (Fig. S2A). It is possible to notice the spherical shape of the synthesized CDs and the small diameter of their particles. Fig. S2B shows the particle size distribution using the DLS technique. According to the results, it is noted that there is an agreement with the results obtained by TEM, with an average diameter of 11 nm.

The optical properties of the CDs were evaluated by the UV–Vis absorption spectra, as can be seen in Fig. S2C. Almost all synthesized CDs from the most diverse techniques have an absorption band in the range of 260–320 nm (Cao et al., 2007). The absorption spectrum shows an absorption peak at 310 nm that can be attributed to the n–π transition of the C‒O (Iqbal et al., 2018) bond and its intensity decreases as the wavelength increases. Other peaks may also appear at shorter wavelengths, associated with π–π* transitions (Wang et al., 2019).

It is noticeable in Fig. S2D that there is an increase in the luminescence intensity of the CDs when there is an increase in the excitation wavelength from 330 to 360, and from 370 there is a decrease in luminescence intensity. This is due to the quantum confinement or size distribution of the CDs (Fan et al., 2017). Based on the results obtained, the 360 nm excitation wavelength was selected for subsequent studies as it has a higher intensity peak emission.

Fig. S3A shows the study of the effect of pH influence on the luminescence signal of CDs in the pH range from 2 to 10. An increase in the luminescence intensity is observed from pH 2 to 3, remaining stable from pH 3 to 5 and decreasing for values above 5. Considering that any pH value from 3 to 5 could be used, pH 4 was chosen.

To study the luminescence stability, the CDs and CDs/MTZ solutions were irradiated for 1 h with a wavelength of 360 nm. As can be seen in the Fig. S3B, both solutions maintained their luminescence stability all the time, showing that they are highly photo-stable and resistant to photodegradation.

The study on the luminescence intensity of the CDs when they are subjected to an increase in the ionic strength of the environment is shown in Fig. S3C. The pH was fixed at 4 with increasing additions of NaCl concentrations, from 0 to 1 mol L−1 in increments of 0.2 mol L−1. No significant change in the luminescence intensity of the CDs was observed when subjected to an increase in the ionic strength of the environment, confirming the CDs high stability.
Using quinine sulfate as a reference and based on Eq. (1), the fluorescence of the CDs was measured at 3.2%.

ce:section.ce:section-title: FTIR CDs, FTIR MTZ and FTIR CDs/MTZ
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 2 shows the FTIR spectra with absorption bands of MTZ (A), CDs/MTZ (B) and CDs (C). The functional groups on the CDs surface have characteristic absorption bands at 3441 cm−1 for O‒H stretching vibrations, 2983–2849 cm−1 for CH stretching vibrations, 1659 cm−1 for C˭C/ C˭O and 1090–985 cm−1 for C‒O‒C/ C‒O. The main MTZ absorption bands are 529 cm−1 for N‒C‒S, 3010 cm−1 for ˭C‒H, 1158–1084 cm−1 for C˭S, 1271 cm−1 for C˭N, 1569 cm−1 for C˭C, 2894 cm−1 for C‒H due to CH3 presence, 1454 cm−1 for CH3 angular deformation and 737 and 3101 cm−1 for N‒H.

To use the synthesized CDs for Hg2+determination, a study was carried out based on the analysis of the fluorescence attenuation decay of the CDs when there are Hg2+ ions and other metals that could be present in the water.

It is noticeable a significant decrease in fluorescence attenuation for Hg2+ e Cu2+ ions when analyzed under the same conditions ( Fig. 3A). Since the results did not show selectivity for the Hg2+ ion, a study was carried out using functionalization to make the nanostructure selective for Hg2+ ion.

As can be seen in Fig. 2A, MTZ has no absorption bands in the spectral window from 3250 to 3750 cm−1, and the CDs chemical compounds Fig. 2C forms a band of great intensity involving the OH functional group, which has its intensity decreased in the presence of MTZ, Fig. 2B. As CDs are extremely electronegative (Fukushima and Aida, 2007; Tunckol et al., 2012), hydrogen bonding maintains this electronegativity by running the hydrogen through the carbon dots (Fukushima and Aida, 2007).

Another evidence of the success in the functionalization of the CDs is shown by the decrease of the band intensity of the asymmetric stretching of the CDs carbonyl groups, in 1659 cm−1, when they interacted with the MTZ through the hydrogen bonds, as shown in the Fig. 2B (de Almeida Barbosa, 2008; Pavia et al., 2013).

Using the FTIR technique, it was possible to verify the CDs functionalization, since it shows characteristic CPZ absorption peaks, such as NH functional groups, which are absent in CDs without functionalization.
Fig. 4 shows the probable CDs functionalization by MTZ, where the MTZ positive end is bonded to the negative end (O‒H) and (˭O) of the CDs surface.

A specific fluorescence quenching effect may be due to the strong interactions between Hg2+ ions and the surface groups of CDs/MTZ, which transferred the photoelectrons from CDs/MTZ to Hg2+ ions. Due to this coordination interaction, the electron in the excited state of the CDs/MTZ is transferred to the unfilled Hg2+ orbit and leads to non-radioactive recombination, which results in the fluorescence quenching (Moreira et al., 2018).

With the verification of functionalization by the FTIR technique, new studies were carried out to verify the improvement in Hg2+ selectivity. The desired result was obtained and shown in Fig. 3B, in which the Cu2+ interference against Hg2+ compared to the non-functionalized CDs was significantly reduced, while other ions had the interference practically eliminated. Also, the zeta potential measured at pH 4 was −17.5 mV, indicating the presence of a negative charge on the CDs surface (Fig. S3D). The negative potential for the CDs is in accordance with functional groups containing oxygen, such as alcohols and carboxylic acids that are electronegative. Therefore, the CDs functionalized with MTZ was decisive for the Hg selectivity and determination.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Application of CDs/MTZ in Hg2+ determination
ce:section.ce:para:
Under the optimization conditions (pH = 4), the CDs-MTZ/ Hg2+ system was studied ( Fig. 5). As shown, fluorescence gradually decreases with the increase of the Hg2+concentration, and the formation of a permanent complex between CDs and Hg2+ ions can lead to the fluorescence quenching (Barman and Sadhukhan, 2012). The calibration curve showed a linear range of 3.3 × 10−7 to 50.0 × 10−6 mol L−1 (I = 2.0 × 108 [Hg2+] + 280; where I is the luminescence intensity and [Hg2+] is the Hg2+ concentration given in µmol L−1). The obtained linear regression presented a high value for the coefficient of determination (0.9978), indicating that the model can explain well the observed values. The limit of detection (LOD) and the limit of quantification (LOQ) were 1.8 × 10−7 mol L−1 and 6.7 × 10−7 mol L−1, respectively. The LOD was calculated using the relation
3 ×
sd
blank
m
and the LOQ was calculated using the relation
10 ×
sd
blank
m
, where
sd
blank
is the standard deviation and m is the slope of the analytical curve.
The precision of the method was studied using solutions of 17 and 30 µmol L−1, obtaining relative standard deviations of 0.1% and 0.3%, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Comparison with other methods to determine Hg2+
ce:section.ce:para: The proposed method presented a lower LOD and a wider linear range when compared with some methods using modified surface nanostructure and equal or lower values than the methods that did not use modifications (Table S3). Considering the easy synthesis of the CDs, the method developed was satisfactory because its values are lower compared to the methods described in the literature.

The method applicability was examined by carrying out recovery experiments on samples of water from a lake, tap, river, and pond. Table 1 shows the results obtained for the Hg2+ concentration levels: 4.0 × 10−6 mol L−1, 17.0 × 10−6 mol L−1 and 30.0 × 10−6 mol L−1. Mean recovery was 100%, indicating the accuracy of the method.

CDs synthesis from rice starch via hydrothermal treatment allowed the formation of particles on a nanometric scale with an average diameter of 11 nm, as evidenced by the DLS technique and spherical morphology, confirmed by the TEM technique. The results obtained by ANOVA, with a 95% confidence level, indicated the temperature as the most influential factor in obtaining highly luminescent CDs. The CDs had excitation/emission maxima of 360/440 nm and a quantum yield of 3.2%. CDs functionalization with MTZ enabled a specific character for Hg2+ determination when in optimal conditions, besides the LOD was 1.8 × 10−7 mol L−1 with a linear range from 3.3 × 10−7 to 50.0 × 10−6 mol L−1 for Hg2+.

The proposed method can be considered a simple, low-cost, selective, and accurate strategy for Hg2+ determination, presenting great potential for natural water samples due to the low toxicity and high sensitivity. In addition, the method answered demands for viable techniques from an economic point of view and simple operational skills for determination of analyte traces in solution.

Maria Carolina Robaina Vieira: Investigation, Methodology, Writing - review & editing. Felipe Andrade Carvalho: Writing - review & editing. Mathews Krambeck de Oliveira Franco: Data curation, Visualization. Willian Toito Suarez: Supervision, Methodology, Writing - review & editing. Castelo Bandane Vilanculo: Methodology, Writing - review & editing. Marcelo Henrique dos Santos: Methodology, Data curation. Francisco Fernandes Gambarra-Neto: Data curation.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors are grateful to the FAPEMIG (Fundação de Amparo à Pesquisa do Estado de Minas Gerais) (Process CEX-APQ-02436-15), Brazil, CAPES (Coordenação de Aperfeiçoamento de Pessoal de Nível Superior), Brazil, CNPq (Conselho Nacional de Desenvolvimento Científico e Tecnológico) and FINEP (Financiadora de Estudos e Projetos), Brazil, for financing the project.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.ecoenv.2021.112043.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
Supplementary material.
